BEFORE THE FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
In the Matter of: 
Opinion requested by: 
w. Dean Cannon, Jr., 
Californians for Fiscal 
Responsibility 
) 
) 
) 
) 
) 
) 
-----------------------------) 
No. 76-001 
August 3, 1976 
BY THE COMMISSION: 
Californians for Fiscal Respon-
sibility (hereinafter CFR), which previously was known as 
the Committee for the Support of ACA 31, provided its member 
savings and loan associations with mailing inserts which 
were included in customer mailings and with counter displays 
ich advocated passage of Proposition 8. 
W. Dean Cannon, 
Treasurer of the Committee, has asked the Commission the 
following questions: 
(1) 
Are payments made by a member association in 
connection with mailing the inserts includable for the pur-
pose of determining whether the member association is a 
"committee" within the meaning of Government Code Section 
82013? 
(2) 
Are payments made by a member association in 
connection with displaying counter signs includable for the 
purpose of determing whether the member association is a 
"committee" within the meaning of Government Code Section 
82013? 
(3) 
Are payments made by a member association for 
the purpose of mailing the inserts and displaying the counter 
signs in-kind contributions to CFR? 
CONCLUSION 
(1) 
Certain payments made in connection with 
mailing the inserts are expenditures and, therefore, are 
includable for the purpose of determining whether a member 
ociat 
n is a "committee" 
in the meaning of Govern-
Code 
e t 
B20l3. 


No. 76-001 
Page Two 
2 FPPC OPINIONS 134 
(2) 
Certain payments made in connection with 
displaying counter signs are expenditures and, therefore, 
are includable for the purpose of determining whether a 
member association is a "committee" within the meaning of 
Government Code Section 82013. 
(3) 
Certain payments made in connection with 
mailing the inserts and displaying the counter signs are in-
kind contributions by the member associations to the CFR. 
Government Code Section 82015. 
ANALYSIS 
(I) 
The first question before us is whether cer-
tain payments made by a member savings and loan association, 
in connection with mailing to its customers political adver-
tisements provided by CFR, are expenditures for the purpose 
of determining whether the member association is a "f?mmittee" 
within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013.-
We 
conclude that they are. 
Section 82013 provides, in pertinent part: 
"Committee" means any person or combination 
of persons who directly or indirectly receives 
contributions or makes expenditures or contri-
butions for the purpose of influencing or 
attempting to influence the action of the 
voters for or against the nomination or 
election of one or more candidates, or the 
passage or defeat of any measure, ••• 
if: 
... 
(b) 
Expenditures and contributions made, 
other than contributions described in sUbsection 
(c), total five hundred dollars ($500) or more 
in a calendar year; ••• 
Section 82025, as amplified by 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18225, provides that any monetary or nonmonetary payment is 
an expenditure if it is made by a candidate, controlled 
committee, committee of a 
litical 
rty, or committee 
organized primarily for polit cal purposes. 
2 Cal. Adm. 
1/ 
All statutory references are to the 
ment Code unless otherwise not 
ern-


No. 76-001 
Page Three 
2 FPPC OPINIONS 135 
Code Section l8225(b). 
With respect to all other persons, a 
monetary or nonmonetary payment is an expenditure if it is 
used to expressly advocate the nomination, election or de-
feat of a clearly identified candidate, or the qualification, 
passage or defeat of a clearly identified measure. 
2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Section 18225(c}. 
In the instant case, the member savings and loan 
associations are not candidates, controlled committees, 
committees of a political party, or groups formed or existing 
primarily for political purposes. 
However, the mailing 
inserts expressly advocate the passage of Proposition 8 and 
payments made in connection with mailing them, therefore, 
are expenditures, unless such payments are specifically 
excluded by other provisions of the Political Reform Act or 
by regulations interpreting those provisions. 
With these general principles as a framework, we 
turn to a consideration of the specific types of payments -
oyee compensation, overhead, and postage costs -
involved 
here. 
First, with respect to compensation paid to an employee 
who assists in the maili 
of the inserts, such payments 
ill be an expenditure if the employee spends 10 percent or 
more of his 
nsated time in a calendar month performing 
services for a political purpose at the request of his employer. 
2 Cal. 
• Code Section 18423(a). 
Subsection (b) of 2 Cal. 
e 
Code Section 18423 
essly provides that "hours spent 
developing or distributing communications that expressly 
vocate the ••• passage or defeat of a clearly identified 
measure" are services provided for a political purpose. 
According ,if an employee of a member association spends 
10 percent or more of his compensated time in a calendar 
month working on the distribution of the mailing inserts, 
the pro rata portion of that employee's compensation attribu-
table to the performance of such services is an expenditure 
which must be counted for the purpose of determining whether 
the member association is a committee. 
However, none of an 
employee's compensation is an expenditure if that employee 
spends less than 10 
[cent of his compensated t 
in a 
calendar month performing political services for his employer. 
With respect to payments for overhead, 2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section l822S(c)(3)(A) provides that expenditures in-
clude any" 
dit 
a1 
inistrative or overhead costs attri-
butable to the communication," but "any of the regular on-
ing business overhead which will be incurred in similar 
amounts regardless of the communication" is expressly ex-
ud 
Thus, 
ents 
r rent, electricity, water, tele-
rat 
cos 
are not reportable ex-
dit 
na 
en s 
r 
ese costs 
e i curred as a result of mailing 
e inserts. 


No. 76-001 
Page Four 
2 FPPC OPINIONS 136 
Finally, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section IB225(c)(3)(A) 
specifically requires reporting of "all costs directly at-
tributable to the communication, including .•. postage ..•. " 
Thus, postage costs attributable to the political communi-
cations involved here are expenditures, regardless of the 
fact that the mailingins~rts will be included in a tegular 
mailing of the member associations. 
Postage costs, unlike other ongoing expenses re-
lated to doing business, are directly tied to the distri-
bution of the communication which expressly advocates a 
position on the ballot measure. 
Moreover, postage costs are 
discrete and, in contrast to normal overhead expenditures, 
it is easy to ascertain the amount attributable to the com-
munication. 
In addition, to exclude postage costs as an 
expenditure when a company includes a political communi-
cation in one of its regular mailings would create an anom-
aly with respect to reportable expenditures as between com-
nies which have regular mailings and those that do not. 
Accordingly, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(c)(3)(A) includes 
postage costs and does not distinguish between 
se situations 
ere the political communication is included in a regular 
mailing 
those where it is not. 
In the 
esent case, 
ere both political and 
nonpolitical material 
11 be included in the mailing, each 
r association must allocate a'reasonable portion of the 
e 
envel 
costs to t 
litical communication 
the purpose of determining the amount of the expenditure 
involved. 
Other than the requirement that any such alloca-
tion be reasonable and be made in good faith, there are no 
precise rules for determining exactly how the allocation 
should be accomplished. 
Generally, however, it will be 
acceptable to allocate on the basis of the comparative number 
of items or pages, or the comparative amount of weight, 
between the political and the nonpolitical materials. 
Thus, to summarize, the expenditures related to 
the mailing inserts which must be considered ex 
nditures 
for the purpose of determining whether a member association 
is a committee include: 
the pro rata portion of salaries 
paid to employees who spend 10 percent or more of their 
compensated time during a calendar month distributing the 
mailing; additional overhead costs incurred because of the 
mailing; and the pro rata portion of postage and envelope 
costs attributable to the mailing. 
If these "expenditures," 
alone or in combination with other "expenditures" or in-kind 
contributions of a member association, total $500 or more in 
cal 
r year, 
e asso 
t 
n is a committee and, conse-
tly, is r 
ir 
to file 
tatements 
rsuant to 
Sections 84200, et s 


No. 76-001 
Page Five 
We also observe that regardless of whether the 
member association is a committee, the provision of Section 
84305 which requires that "a copy of every mass mailing in 
support of or in opposition to a ••• state measure shall be 
sent to the Commission on the same day on which it is mailed 
to the public generally" inust2~e followed. 
"Mass mailing" 
is de~}ned in Section 82041.5-
and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18241-
as 200 or more identical or nearly identical pieces 
of mail sent in a calendar month. 
Accordingly, if 200 or 
more of these mailing inserts are sent by a member associ-
ation in a 
cal~?dar month, this provision of Section 84305 
is applicable.-
In addition, if a member association is a 
committee and the inserts are a mass mailing, such inserts 
must be sent by bulk rate or postage meter, or the name and 
street address of the sender must appear on the outside of 
the envelope and on the inserts themselves. 
(2) 
The second question we have been asked is 
whe 
r costs incurred in connection with displaying counter 
signs advocating the passage of Proposition 8 are "expendi-
tures" which must be incl 
for the purpose of determining 
er a member association is a committee pursuant to 
Section 82013. 
Since the counter signs were provided by 
CFR, the mem 
r associations did not incur any costs in 
connection 
i 
the construction or development of the signs. 
i 
r 
ct to whether employee salaries or overhead costs 
incurr 
in connection with setting 
and maintaining the 
counter displays are ex 
nditures, the standards set forth 
in part 1 of this opinion are applicable. 
]j 
Section 82041.5 states: 
"Mass mailing" means two hundred or more identical 
or nearly identical pieces of mail, but does not 
include a form letter or other mail which is sent 
in response to a request, letter or other inquiry. 
1/ 
2 Cal. Adm Code Section 18241 states: 
For the purposes of Government Code Section 82041.5, 
a "mass mailing" has been made when 200 or more 
identical, or nearly i entical, pieces of mail 
have been sent within a calendar month. 
if 
Since all of the mailing inserts sent by the 
various member associat 
flce for purposes of 
ns will 
identical, it will suf-
liance wi 
ction 84305 if the 
r 
sane 
the 
i 
insert to the Commission. 


No. 76-001 
Page Six 
2 FPPC OPINIONS 138 
(3) 
The final question before the Commission is 
whether payments made by a member association in connection 
with mailing the inserts or displaying the counter signs are 
in-kind contributions by the member associations to CFR. 
Section 82015, as interpreted by 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18215, provides that a contributiori includes any 
monetary or nonmonetary payment, not supported by full and 
adequate consideration, which is received by or made at the 
behest of a candidate, a controlled committee, an official 
committee of a political party, or an organization formed or 
existing primarily for political purposes. 
"Made at the 
behest of" means a payment "made under the control or at the 
direction of a candidate, controlled committee, official 
committee of a political party, or organization formed or 
existing primarily for political purposes." 
2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section l8215(c). 
In the instant case, CFR was formed primarily for 
the political purpose of influencing the voters to adopt 
Proposition 8. 
Moreover, CFR controlled and directed pay-
ments by its member associations in two respects. 
First, 
CFR designed and print 
the mailing inserts and counter 
displays and thus controlled the content of the communi-
cations. 
Second, it coordinated the activities of its mem-
r associations relative to the mailings and displays. 
We 
conclude, therefore, that to the extent payments by member 
associat 
ns in connection with distributing the inserts or 
displaying the counter signs are "contributions," they are 
in-kind contributions to CFR. 
An employee's salary is a contribution only if the 
employee "spends more than 10 percent of his compensated 
time in anyone month rendering services for political pur-
poses." 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18423(a). 
Accordingly, 
employee compensation will be an in-kind contribution in the 
present case only if the time threshold is surpassed in 
connection with mailing the inserts and displaying the counter 
signs. 
Moreover, since payments for r 
ular business over-
head are not reportable expenditures by a member association 
for the purposes of determining whether 
e association is a 
committee, we conclude that such 
ents also are not re-
portable as an in-kind contri 
tion by the recipient CFR. 
Al 
ugh there is no express exclusion for regular ongoing 
bu iness overhead from what constitutes an in-kind contri-
bution, no legitimate purpose would be served by deeming 
such expenses a contribution but not an expenditure. 
To the 
contrary, to 
0 so would create unnecessary accounting burdens 
r the 
ember asso 
at 
would 
re the reasoning 
ich result 
in ex 
costs from 
rtable 
ditures. 
Finally, with r 
to 
stage 
envelope 


No. 76-001 
Page Seven 
2 FPPC OPINIONS 139 
costs, these will be in-kind contributions to CFR, and the 
amount of such contributions will be equal to the amount of 
the postage costs which is an expenditure. 
In conclusion, 
therefore, a member association's in-kind contribution to 
CFR will be identical to its expenditures in connection with 
distributing the inserts and displaying the counter signs. 
Adopted by the Commission August 3, 1976. 
Concur-
ring: 
Brosnahan, Lapan, Lowenstein and Quinn. 
Commissioner 
Carpenter dissented.

December 31, 1975 
Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, Calif. 
95814 
Dear Mr. Lowenstein: 
The Committee for the Support of ACA 31 respectfully requests 
that the Chairman render, at his earliest convenience, an 
advisory opinion with regard to the following course of action 
intended by the Committee: 
I. 
The Committee proposes to furnish its member savings 
and loan association§'with mail 
inserts urging the 
recipient to vote in favor of ACA 31, a statewide ballot 
measure~ These inserts would then be included by the 
member associations in regular mailings to customers 
which would be made regardless of 't"hether the insert is 
included. , The Corr~ittee requests the Chairman's opinion 
on the following questions: 
1) 
Is a member association's effort and expense an 
"in-kind" contribution to the committee, or is it 
an "expenditure" which, if in excess of $500 would 
classify that association as a "committee." 
2) 
Whether an "expenditure," or an "in-kind" contribution, 
't"hat is the value or cost factor attributable to inclusion 
of the insert in a regular customer mailing which would 
be made in any event? 
II. 
The Committee also proposes to print signs for counter 
display in the offices of member associations, where. 
they would be vie\ved by customers but not by the general 
public. 
Since there is no known instance' of a savings 
and loan association renting counter space, how is the 
use of such SDace valued? 
Is the p1ace~ent of such a 
sign by a me:n:;er association an "in-kir .. !,. contribution, 
or an "expenditure," and how would the cost factor or value 
be determined for reporting purposes. 


III. 
The Committee proposes to furnish member associations with 
window signs which can be viewed by members of the general 
public who pass in front of an office window in \.;hich such 
a sign has been placed. 
There is no knmm instance of a 
savings and loan association renting tvindow space, so how is 
the value or cost factor of the use of such space calculated? 
Is the placement of window sign an "expenditure," or an 
"in-kind" contribution to the Committee? 
Because authorization applications for the second authorization must 
be filed by January 23, 1976, it is respectfully requested that the 
Chairman render his opinion as soon as possible, so that the Committee 
can make the necessary calculations which are contingent thereon. 
Sincerely,

existing primarily for political purposes.

committee of a political party, or organization formed or 
existing primarily for political purposes." 
2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section l8215(c).

cations. 
Second, it coordinated the activities of its mem-
r associations relative to the mailings and displays. 
We 
conclude, therefore, that to the extent payments by member 
associat 
ns in connection with distributing the inserts or 
displaying the counter signs are "contributions," they are 
in-kind contributions to CFR. 
An employee's salary is a contribution only if the 
employee "spends more than 10 percent of his compensated 
time in anyone month rendering services for political pur-
poses." 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18423(a). 
Accordingly, 
employee compensation will be an in-kind contribution in the 
present case only if the time threshold is surpassed in 
connection with mailing the inserts and displaying the counter 
signs. 
Moreover, since payments for r 
ular business over-
head are not reportable expenditures by a member association 
for the purposes of determining whether 
e association is a 
committee, we conclude that such 
ents also are not re-
portable as an in-kind contri 
tion by the recipient CFR. 
Al 
ugh there is no express exclusion for regular ongoing 
bu iness overhead from what constitutes an in-kind contri-
bution, no legitimate purpose would be served by deeming 
such expenses a contribution but not an expenditure. 
To the 
contrary, to 
0 so would create unnecessary accounting burdens 
r the 
ember asso 
at 
would 
re the reasoning 
ich result 
in ex 
costs from 
rtable 
ditures. 
Finally, with r 
to 
stage 
envelope 


No. 76-001 
Page Seven 
2 FPPC OPINIONS 139 
costs, these will be in-kind contributions to CFR, and the 
amount of such contributions will be equal to the amount of 
the postage costs which is an expenditure. 
In conclusion, 
therefore, a member association's in-kind contribution to 
CFR will be identical to its expenditures in connection with 
distributing the inserts and displaying the counter signs. 
Adopted by the Commission August 3, 1976. 
Concur-
ring: 
Brosnahan, Lapan, Lowenstein and Quinn. 
Commissioner 
Carpenter dissented.

December 31, 1975 
Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman 
 
Dear Mr. Lowenstein: 
The Committee for the Support of ACA 31 respectfully requests 
that the Chairman render, at his earliest convenience, an 
advisory opinion with regard to the following course of action 
intended by the Committee: 
I. 
The Committee proposes to furnish its member savings 
and loan association§'with mail 
inserts urging the 
recipient to vote in favor of ACA 31, a statewide ballot 
measure~ These inserts would then be included by the 
member associations in regular mailings to customers 
which would be made regardless of 't"hether the insert is 
included. , The Corr~ittee requests the Chairman's opinion 
on the following questions: 
1) 
Is a member association's effort and expense an 
"in-kind" contribution to the committee, or is it 
an "expenditure" which, if in excess of $500 would 
classify that association as a "committee." 
2) 
Whether an "expenditure," or an "in-kind" contribution, 
't"hat is the value or cost factor attributable to inclusion 
of the insert in a regular customer mailing which would 
be made in any event? 
II. 
The Committee also proposes to print signs for counter 
display in the offices of member associations, where. 
they would be vie\ved by customers but not by the general 
public. 
Since there is no known instance' of a savings 
and loan association renting counter space, how is the 
use of such SDace valued? 
Is the p1ace~ent of such a 
sign by a me:n:;er association an "in-kir .. !,. contribution, 
or an "expenditure," and how would the cost factor or value 
be determined for reporting purposes. 


III. 
The Committee proposes to furnish member associations with 
window signs which can be viewed by members of the general 
public who pass in front of an office window in \.;hich such 
a sign has been placed. 
There is no knmm instance of a 
savings and loan association renting tvindow space, so how is 
the value or cost factor of the use of such space calculated? 
Is the placement of window sign an "expenditure," or an 
"in-kind" contribution to the Committee? 
Because authorization applications for the second authorization must 
be filed by January 23, 1976, it is respectfully requested that the 
Chairman render his opinion as soon as possible, so that the Committee 
can make the necessary calculations which are contingent thereon.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether payments made by member savings and loan associations for mailing political advertisement inserts and displaying counter signs advocating Proposition 8 constitute reportable expenditures and in-kind contributions under Political Reform Act provisions defining "committees" and contributions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that certain payments by member associations for mailing inserts and displaying counter signs constitute reportable expenditures under the $500 threshold for committee status, including pro rata employee compensation (if 10% or more of time), additional overhead costs, and postage/envelope costs. These payments also constitute in-kind contributions to CFR when made at CFR's behest.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document is FPPC Opinion No. 76-001 from August 3, 1976. Clear Q&C sections with detailed analysis. Text partially truncated at end but all major question and conclusion sections extracted. Some OCR artifacts present but content clearly legible.

QUESTION: (1) Are payments made by a member association in connection with mailing the inserts includable for the purpose of determining whether the member association is a "committee" within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013? (2) Are payments made by a member association in connection with displaying counter signs includable for the purpose of determing whether the member association is a "committee" within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013? (3) Are payments made by a member association for the purpose of mailing the inserts and displaying the counter signs in-kind contributions to CFR?

CONCLUSION: (1) 
Certain payments made in connection with 
mailing the inserts are expenditures and, therefore, are 
includable for the purpose of determining whether a member 
ociat 
n is a "committee" 
in the meaning of Govern-

(2)

embedding.first_500_words: BEFORE THE FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION In the Matter of: Opinion requested by: w. Dean Cannon, Jr., Californians for Fiscal Responsibility ) ) ) ) ) ) -----------------------------) No. 76-001 August 3, 1976 BY THE COMMISSION: Californians for Fiscal Respon- sibility (hereinafter CFR), which previously was known as the Committee for the Support of ACA 31, provided its member savings and loan associations with mailing inserts which were included in customer mailings and with counter displays ich advocated passage of Proposition 8. W. Dean Cannon, Treasurer of the Committee, has asked the Commission the following questions: (1) Are payments made by a member association in connection with mailing the inserts includable for the pur- pose of determining whether the member association is a "committee" within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013? (2) Are payments made by a member association in connection with displaying counter signs includable for the purpose of determing whether the member association is a "committee" within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013? (3) Are payments made by a member association for the purpose of mailing the inserts and displaying the counter signs in-kind contributions to CFR? CONCLUSION (1) Certain payments made in connection with mailing the inserts are expenditures and, therefore, are includable for the purpose of determining whether a member ociat n is a "committee" in the meaning of Govern- Code e t B20l3. No. 76-001 Page Two 2 FPPC OPINIONS 134 (2) Certain payments made in connection with displaying counter signs are expenditures and, therefore, are includable for the purpose of determining whether a member association is a "committee" within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013. (3) Certain payments made in connection with mailing the inserts and displaying the counter signs are in- kind contributions by the member associations to the CFR. Government Code Section 82015. ANALYSIS (I) The first question before us is whether cer- tain payments made by a member savings and loan association, in connection with mailing to its customers political adver- tisements provided by CFR, are expenditures for the purpose of determining whether the member association is a "f?mmittee" within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013.- We conclude that they are. Section 82013 provides, in pertinent part: "Committee" means any person or combination of persons who directly or indirectly receives contributions or makes expenditures or contri- butions for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of one or more candidates, or the passage or defeat of any measure, ••• if: ... (b) Expenditures and contributions made, other than contributions described in sUbsection (c), total five hundred dollars ($500) or more in a calendar year; ••• Section 82025, as amplified by 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225, provides that any monetary or nonmonetary payment is an expenditure if it is made by a candidate, controlled committee, committee of a litical rty, or committee organized primarily for polit cal purposes. 2 Cal. Adm. 1/ All statutory
embedding.summary: The FPPC addresses whether payments by savings and loan associations for distributing political materials advocating Proposition 8 are reportable expenditures and contributions. The Commission concludes that pro rata employee salaries, additional overhead, and postage costs must be counted toward the $500 committee threshold, and such payments constitute in-kind contributions to the sponsoring organization.
